exercises.title: Classify chaos assaults by risk level
exercises.instructions:
Rank these Chaos Monkey assaults (1=safest, 4=most dangerous) and explain when to use each:
A: LatencyAssault (adds 500ms-3s to method calls)
B: ExceptionAssault (throws RuntimeException randomly)
C: KillAppAssault (crashes the JVM process)
D: MemoryAssault (allocates memory until near OOM)
exercises.expected_solution:
1. SAFEST: A — LatencyAssault
   Adds artificial delay, does not cause data loss or crashes
   Slowest methods still return correct results (eventually)
   Tests: circuit breakers open on timeouts, fallbacks activated
   Good for: staging AND production (with monitoring)
   When: verify timeout configurations, CB thresholds

2. MODERATE: B — ExceptionAssault
   Throws RuntimeException from random method calls
   Tests: exception handling, circuit breaker on error rate
   Risk: if fallback is broken, requests fail visibly
   Good for: staging; production only with very low level (1 in 100)
   When: verify @CircuitBreaker, @Fallback, error handling paths

3. DANGEROUS: D — MemoryAssault
   Allocates large byte arrays, causes GC pressure and OOM
   Tests: memory limits, OOM handling, graceful degradation
   Risk: can cause legitimate OOM crashes and data loss
   Good for: staging only; never on production without autoscaling
   When: verify JVM heap settings, memory leak detection

4. MOST DANGEROUS: C — KillAppAssault
   Crashes the entire JVM process immediately
   Tests: pod restart time, k8s liveness probe, auto-restart
   Risk: any in-flight requests are lost; data corruption possible
   Good for: staging only; production only with full chaos team + runbooks
   When: verify k8s auto-restart, load balancer failover

test_cases.expected:
1. SAFEST: A — LatencyAssault
   Adds artificial delay, does not cause data loss or crashes
   Slowest methods still return correct results (eventually)
   Tests: circuit breakers open on timeouts, fallbacks activated
   Good for: staging AND production (with monitoring)
   When: verify timeout configurations, CB thresholds

2. MODERATE: B — ExceptionAssault
   Throws RuntimeException from method calls
   Tests: exception handling, circuit breaker on error rate
   Risk: if fallback is broken, requests fail visibly
   Good for: staging; production only with very low level (1 in 100)
   When: verify @CircuitBreaker, @Fallback, error handling paths

3. DANGEROUS: D — MemoryAssault
   Allocates large byte arrays, causes GC pressure and OOM
   Tests: memory limits, OOM handling, graceful degradation
   Risk: can cause legitimate OOM crashes and data loss
   Good for: staging only; never on production without autoscaling
   When: verify JVM heap settings, memory leak detection

4. MOST DANGEROUS: C — KillAppAssault
   Crashes the entire JVM process immediately
   Tests: pod restart time, k8s liveness probe, auto-restart
   Risk: any in-flight requests are lost; data corruption possible
   Good for: staging only; production only with full chaos team + runbooks
   When: verify k8s auto-restart, load balancer failover

hints: Latency: safest — data integrity maintained
KillApp: most dangerous — always start with latency first
Production chaos: start minimal, have runbooks ready

exercises.title: Complete the Chaos Monkey application.yml
exercises.instructions: Fill in the Chaos Monkey configuration to assault services with latency.
exercises.starter_code:
chaos:
  monkey:
    enabled: ___
    watcher:
      service: ___  # Assault @Service beans
    assaults:
      level: ___      # 1 in 5 calls assaulted
      ___-active: true
      latency-range-start: 500
      latency-range-end: 2000
exercises.expected_solution:
chaos:
  monkey:
    enabled: true
    watcher:
      service: true  # Assault @Service beans
    assaults:
      level: 5        # 1 in 5 calls assaulted
      latency-active: true
      latency-range-start: 500
      latency-range-end: 2000

blanks.hint: 1 in 5 calls will be assaulted (level 1 = every call, level 10 = 1 in 10)

hints: level: 5 means 1 in 5 calls assaulted (not 5%)
watcher.service: true targets all @Service beans
latency-active: false by default — must explicitly enable

exercises.title: Steady-state hypothesis definition
exercises.instructions:
Define steady-state hypothesis for Razorpay payment API:
1. SteadyState record: p99Ms, errorRatePct, cbState
2. SteadyStateMonitor: captureSnapshot(), isInSteadyState(snapshot)
3. Hypothesis: 'Even under HDFC 3s latency, error rate < 5%, p99 < 2000ms'
4. @SpringBootTest: snapshot captured, hypothesis evaluated
hints: Steady state: pre-chaos baseline (strict SLA)
Hypothesis: chaos-state SLA (more lenient — fallbacks running)
CB state OPEN: expected during chaos (proves CB activated)

exercises.title: @EnableChaosMonkey configuration class
exercises.instructions:
Write a Spring Boot chaos engineering setup:
1. @EnableChaosMonkey application (test profile only)
2. ChaosProperties @ConfigurationProperties for toggling assaults
3. @TestConfiguration: Chaos Monkey only active when spring.profiles.active=chaos
4. @SpringBootTest: chaos not active by default, active with 'chaos' profile
hints: @Profile('chaos'): @Configuration only active with that Spring profile
application-chaos.yml: properties file for chaos profile
Chaos disabled by default: prevents accidental production chaos

exercises.title: Chaos experiment: circuit breaker under latency assault
exercises.instructions:
Write a chaos experiment verifying circuit breaker behavior:
1. ChaosExperiment: injects latency via a mock 'chaotic' service
2. CircuitBreakerMonitor: checks CB state
3. Run N calls with high latency → CB should be non-CLOSED
4. Verify fallback counter increased
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): normal→CB closed, chaos→CB opens
hints: throw RuntimeException in chaos mode: simulates gateway failure without real chaos monkey
fallbackCount.incrementAndGet(): track fallback invocations for assertion
@BeforeEach: reset chaos state between tests

exercises.title: Chaos Monkey REST API usage
exercises.instructions:
Design Chaos Monkey actuator REST API calls for a chaos experiment script:
1. GET /actuator/chaosmonkey — check current chaos config
2. POST /actuator/chaosmonkey/assaults — enable latency assault
3. Monitor: GET /actuator/metrics/resilience4j.circuitbreaker.state
4. Disable: POST /actuator/chaosmonkey (enabled=false)
5. @SpringBootTest: ChaosApiClient methods documented
hints: Chaos Monkey actuator: requires management.endpoint.chaosmonkey.enabled=true
level: 1 = every call assaulted (strongest for testing)
Disable between tests: always clean up chaos state

exercises.title: Resilience verification under chaos
exercises.instructions:
Write ChaosResilienceVerifier:
1. simulateChaos(serviceName, failureRatePct): injects failures
2. verifyCircuitBreakerOpened(name): checks CB state != CLOSED
3. verifyFallbackActivated(name): checks fallback counter > 0
4. verifyRecovery(name): checks CB returns to CLOSED after chaos stops
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): chaos injects, CB checked, fallback verified
hints: ≥5 failures → CB OPEN: simplified threshold for unit test
verifyRecovery: reset CB state to CLOSED (chaos stopped)
Three assertions: CB opened + fallback activated + recovery — all required

exercises.title: Chaos experiment pipeline
exercises.instructions:
Write a ChaosExperimentPipeline:
1. Step 1: captureBaseline() — record steady-state metrics
2. Step 2: injectChaos() — enable assault
3. Step 3: runLoad() — send N requests during chaos
4. Step 4: observe() — check CB + fallback + p99
5. Step 5: validateHypothesis() — assert hypothesis holds
6. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): pipeline passes when hypothesis met, fails when not
hints: Hypothesis as record: explicit, immutable, testable
CB opens → p99 is low (fallback fast) + error rate is low (fallback works)
CB stays closed → p99 spikes + error rate spikes → hypothesis violated

exercises.title: Chaos for database failure resilience
exercises.instructions:
Simulate and verify database failure chaos:
1. DatabaseChaosSimulator: can throw DataAccessException
2. PaymentService: @CircuitBreaker + fallback for DB calls
3. Chaos test: DB fails for 20 calls → CB opens → fallback returns cached data
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): DB chaos → fallback, recovery after chaos
hints: TransientDataAccessException: Spring's DB failure exception (triggers CB)
Fallback cached data: graceful degradation (stale but available)
@BeforeEach reset: clean chaos state between tests

exercises.title: Chaos experiment analysis
exercises.instructions:
This chaos experiment ran against Razorpay payment service:

```
Hypothesis: 'If HDFC gateway adds 3s latency, the circuit breaker opens
and fallback activates within 10 calls. Overall p99 stays < 1000ms.'

Chaos injected: LatencyAssault 3000ms on HdfcGatewayService.charge()

Observations after 25 calls:
  CB state: CLOSED (unexpected!)
  Fallback invocations: 0
  p99: 3200ms (SLA breached)
  Error rate: 0%
```

Why did the hypothesis fail? What are 3 possible root causes and fixes?
exercises.expected_solution:
THE HYPOTHESIS FAILED because the circuit breaker did NOT open, despite 3s latency.

ROOT CAUSE 1: CircuitBreaker configured for slow_call_rate_threshold only
  CB config: minimumNumberOfCalls=10, slowCallRateThreshold=50%
  Problem: calls are NOT failing (no exceptions thrown) — they're just SLOW
  Slow call threshold: a 3s call IS considered 'slow' by Resilience4j
  But if slowCallDurationThreshold is set > 3s: the 3s call is NOT counted as slow!
  Fix: `slowCallDurationThreshold: 1s` (calls > 1s = slow → contribute to threshold)

ROOT CAUSE 2: Not enough calls to trigger threshold
  minimumNumberOfCalls=50, but only 25 calls ran
  CB evaluates after minimum window is reached
  Fix: reduce minimumNumberOfCalls to 10 for testing, or run more calls

ROOT CAUSE 3: Latency assault not targeting the right bean
  Chaos Monkey watcher might not be watching HdfcGatewayService
  If watcher.service=false or the class is not @Service: no assault applied
  The 3s latency is from Chaos Monkey annotation, but service might be a @Component
  Fix: check `GET /actuator/chaosmonkey/watchers` — verify service is listed
  Fix: add @ChaosMonkeyWatcher explicitly on HdfcGatewayService

GENERAL LESSON:
  Error rate = 0%: requests succeeded (just slowly) — no exception-based CB trigger
  CB needs: either slowCallRateThreshold OR failureRateThreshold to trigger
  p99 = 3200ms: user-visible impact despite CB not opening
  The chaos test CORRECTLY identified a gap in the resilience configuration!

  Slow call threshold: a 3s call IS considered 'slow' by Resilience4j
  But if slowCallDurationThreshold is set > 3s: the 3s call is NOT counted as slow!
  Fix: `slowCallDurationThreshold: 1s` (calls > 1s = slow → contribute to threshold)

ROOT CAUSE 2: Not enough calls to trigger threshold
  minimumNumberOfCalls=50, but only 25 calls ran
  CB evaluates after minimum window is reached
  Fix: reduce minimumNumberOfCalls to 10 for testing, or run more calls

ROOT CAUSE 3: Latency assault not targeting the right bean
  Chaos Monkey watcher might not be watching HdfcGatewayService
  If watcher.service=false or the class is not @Service: no assault applied
  The 3s latency is from Chaos Monkey annotation, but service might be a @Component
  Fix: check `GET /actuator/chaosmonkey/watchers` — verify service is listed

GENERAL LESSON:
  Error rate = 0%: requests succeeded (just slowly) — no exception-based CB trigger
  CB needs: either slowCallRateThreshold OR failureRateThreshold to trigger
  p99 = 3200ms: user-visible impact despite CB not opening
  The chaos test CORRECTLY identified a gap in the resilience configuration!

hints: Slow calls: 3s latency = slow, not failed — CB needs slowCallDurationThreshold
minimumNumberOfCalls: CB waits for window before evaluating
Watcher not configured: chaos assault never actually injected

exercises.title: Complete chaos verification test suite
exercises.instructions:
Build complete chaos verification for Razorpay:
1. PaymentService with @CircuitBreaker for HDFC
2. ChaosTestHelper: injectLatencyFailures(serviceName, count)
3. Verify: CB state, fallback counter, p99 within threshold
4. Verify: recovery after chaos stops
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): all pass
hints: @BeforeEach reset(): clean state between tests
FALLBACK: prefix on result: verifies fallback code ran
After chaos disabled: CB may still be OPEN (half-open retry), but fallback still handles

exercises.title: Game day planning document
exercises.instructions:
Write a GameDayPlanner service for Razorpay:
1. GameDayExperiment: name, hypothesis, chaosType, blast radius, rollback procedure
2. GameDayRunner: validate hypothesis met after experiment
3. 3 experiments: HDFC latency, DB exception, Kafka consumer lag
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): valid experiment, hypothesis validation
hints: GameDayExperiment.isValid(): validate experiment is well-defined before running
Hypothesis must contain 'When': enforces proper hypothesis format
LATENCY chaos: requires BOTH CB opened AND fallback worked

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay chaos engineering suite
exercises.instructions:
Build complete chaos engineering suite:
1. 3 experiments: HDFC latency, payment DB, notification service down
2. For each: steady state → hypothesis → inject → observe → validate
3. ChaosReport: tracks pass/fail per experiment
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): each experiment type validates correctly
hints: p99=450ms < 1000ms threshold + fallbacks=15>0 → PASS
fallbacks=0 with requiresFallbacks → FAIL (notification has no fallback)
ChaosOutcome: immutable — stores result for reporting

exercises.title: Chaos-driven TDD for resilience
exercises.instructions:
Use chaos results to drive test and code improvements:
1. DiscoveredGap record: from chaos experiment (what broke)
2. ResilienceImprovement: the fix (CB config change, fallback addition)
3. ChaosRegressionTest: re-run chaos after fix to verify improvement
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): gap identified, fix applied, regression passes
hints: DiscoveredGap: documents the full chaos finding (observation + root cause + fix)
getOpenGaps: filter by fixesApplied map
verifyChaosRegression: requires fix to be applied AND chaos to now pass

exercises.title: Complete M25 T5 Chaos Engineering synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M25 T5 synthesis:
1. PaymentService with @CircuitBreaker (chaotic HDFC + fallback)
2. SteadyStateMonitor: baseline + chaos metrics
3. ChaosHypothesis: validate with p99, fallback, CB assertions
4. GameDayRunner: list of experiments
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): chaos activates fallback, hypothesis met, gap tracked, recovery
6. All 4 pass
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M25 T5 Chaos Engineering synthesis
hints: reset() before each test: clean fallback counter + chaos state
requireFallback=true + 0 fallbacks → hypothesis fails
After chaos disabled: CircuitBreaker may be OPEN but call succeeds via new CB window
